coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based modularization fluorescent probe for simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in water, cells and zebrafish
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yunhao
Shi, Jiahui
Ning, Juan
Hu, Guizhen
Zhou, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: Modular assembly of carbon dots with organic luminescent small molecules can be devoted to picking up a predetermined fluorescent probe detection system for specific targets. In this work, the blue fluorescent carbon dots B-CDs were prepared by using sodium citrate and nitrogen-rich branched polyethyleneimine as precursors through a functional preservation strategy. A modularized, difunctional and ratiometric fluorescent probe B-CDs@RBH with dual-emission was dexterously prepared by simply blending B-CDs and rhodamine b-hydrazide for the detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+. B-CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ through static quenching, while Cu2+ can specifically open the lactam of rhodamine b-hydrazide to form rhodamine B through hydrolysis accompanied by a turn-on orange fluorescence. More interestingly, rhodamine B can absorb the fluorescence of B-CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer, resulting in a blue to orange fluorescence color change in the presence of Cu2+. B-CDs@RBH has a low detection limit of 17.79 nM and 4.19 nM for Hg2+ and Cu2+ respectively with a detection range of 0.1–5 μM, which was applied for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish, and achieved smartphone-assisted on-site water samples detection. The facile modular design of B-CDs@RBH fluorescent probe and convenient on-site detection application are of great benefit in further ascertaining the migration and transformation of heavy metals in biological and environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Modularization
Hg2+ and Cu2+
Carbon dots
Smartphone-assisted method
Functional preservation strategy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of vision detection sensing mechanism
Optimal working conditions of bifunctional fluorescent probes
The fluorescence response of B-CDs@RBH to Hg2+ and Cu2+
Smartphone application for the detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+
Imaging of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs in the presence or absence of Hg2+. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of RBH in the presence/absence of Cu2+. (c) Spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of B-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of RB and RBH. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs in the presence or absence of RBH and Cu2+.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum, (b) linear relationship and (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs@RBH in the presence of Hg2+. (d) Fluorescence spectrum, (e) linear relationship and (f) UV–vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs@RBH in the presence of Cu2+. λex = 355 nm.
The selectivity and anti-interference of B-CDs@RBH for (a) Hg2+ and (b) Cu2+. 1–15 are blank, Mg2+, Ba2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively.

(a) The relationship between Hg2+ (b) Cu2+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@RBH solution and color change. The illustration shows photos taken with a smartphone under a 365 nm UV light.

Confocal images of A549 cells were pretreated with (a) 0 μM, (b) 1 μM, (c) 3 μM, (d) 5 μM Hg2+ for 30 min and then incubated with B-CDs@RBH for 1 h. (e) Relative average fluorescence intensity in blue channel measured by Image J. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). λex = 405 nm, collected at 420–580 nm. The scale is 20 μm.

Confocal images of A549 cells were pretreated with (a) 0 μM, (b) 1 μM, (c) 3 μM, (d) 5 μM Cu2+ for 30 min and then incubated with B-CDs@RBH for 1 h. (e) Relative average fluorescence intensity in blue channel and orange channel measured by Image J. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Blue channel: λex = 405 nm, collected at 420–580 nm. Orange channel: λex = 488 nm, collected at 550–620 nm. The scale is 20 μm.
Schematic diagram of the dual-function fluorescent probe and its application as Hg2+ and Cu2+ probe.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based modularization fluorescent probe for simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in water, cells and zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modular assembly of carbon dots with organic luminescent small molecules can be devoted to picking up a predetermined fluorescent probe detection system for specific targets. In this work, the blue fluorescent carbon dots B-CDs were prepared by using sodium citrate and nitrogen-rich branched polyethyleneimine as precursors through a functional preservation strategy. A modularized, difunctional and ratiometric fluorescent probe B-CDs@RBH with dual-emission was dexterously prepared by simply blending B-CDs and rhodamine b-hydrazide for the detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+. B-CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ through static quenching, while Cu2+ can specifically open the lactam of rhodamine b-hydrazide to form rhodamine B through hydrolysis accompanied by a turn-on orange fluorescence. More interestingly, rhodamine B can absorb the fluorescence of B-CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer, resulting in a blue to orange fluorescence color change in the presence of Cu2+. B-CDs@RBH has a low detection limit of 17.79 nM and 4.19 nM for Hg2+ and Cu2+ respectively with a detection range of 0.1–5 μM, which was applied for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish, and achieved smartphone-assisted on-site water samples detection. The facile modular design of B-CDs@RBH fluorescent probe and convenient on-site detection application are of great benefit in further ascertaining the migration and transformation of heavy metals in biological and environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive heavy metal ions such as Hg2+ and Cu2+ in the environment are not only difficult to be biodegraded, but also enriched into the human body through the food chain under the action of biomagnification, thereby damaging biological organs, nervous system and digestive system [1–11]. Therefore, the development of sensitive and highly selective sensors to conveniently detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ is of great significance to environmental protection and human health.

Currently, there are numerous analytical techniques for the detection of Hg2+ or Cu2+, such as spectrophotometry, electrochemical methods, atomic spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, and chromatography [12–16]. Fluorescence-based methods are widely used due to their quick response, high sensitivity, low cost, and rapid non-destructive imaging [17]. Although various ratiometric fluorescent probes and sensors for Hg2+ or Cu2+ detection are emerging, most of them require complex synthesis and purification steps and can only detect a single target analyte via a single emission mode. What's more, samples need to be taken back to the laboratory for off-site measurement on a fluorescence spectrometer, which is easily affected by environmental conditions such as the instrument and the sample [18–22]. In consequence, the preparation of a transparent ratiometric probe and the on-site determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ are still facing great challenges.

As a nano fluorescent material with superior biocompatibility, carbon dots with precursor-related functional groups on the surface can be retained through the precursor functional preservation strategy [23]. Modular assembly of carbon dots with organic luminescent small molecules can be applied to prepare a predetermined bifunctional fluorescent probe detection system for specific targets. Nowadays, the portable, intuitive smartphones with high-resolution and sensitive cameras are widely used in the fields of on-site applications as they can be installed by a variety of third-party service providers to achieve their powerful functions. Accordingly, the application of ratiometric fluorescent probes for the simultaneous on-site determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ with the aid of a smartphone has an excellent practical application prospect.

Herein, we take advantage of the excellent luminescence properties of carbon dots prepared using a functional preservation strategy, and combine carbon dots as nanocarriers with rhodamine b-hydrazide (RBH), a common rhodamine derivative, to form a bifunctional fluorescent probe to detect Hg2+ and Cu2+. After simple blending of B-CDs and RBH, a bifunctional FRET-type ratiometric fluorescent probe B-CDs@RBH with simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ was obtained with no complicated synthesis process and surface modification. B-CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ through static electron transfer, while RBH contains a lactamide group, which can be hydrolyzed by Cu2+ to open the loop to form rhodamine B (RB), resulting in a turn-on orange fluorescence [24]. Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of RB overlaps with the emission spectrum of B-CDs, RB absorbs the fluorescence energy of B-CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and resulting in a more easily discernible fluorescent color shift from blue to orange. Through the dual emission bands self-calibration, the background interference was effectively reduced. The detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish was realized using B-CDs@RBH, indicating its potential application in the biomedical field. In addition, the on-site detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ was accomplished by using smartphone color recognition software to identify the fluorescent color RGB values of the probe solution.

Sodium citrate, RB and branched polyethyleneimine (M.W. 1800, 99%) (bPEI) were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., LTD. All metal salts were purchased from Energy Chemical Co., LTD. Tetramethylazazole blue (MTT) was purchased from Shanghai Sigma Trading Co., LTD. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were acquired from U-4100 spectrophotometer and Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer respectively, and fluorescence attenuation curves were obtained by Edinburgh FLS980. The HRTEM and TEM images were collected on JEM-2100. Cell images were obtained by laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus FV3000). Nikon TI2-U inverted fluorescence microscope was used for zebrafish imaging.

bPEI (0.5 g) and sodium citrate (0.7 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. After ultrasonic for 10 min, it was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reaction kettle and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. The color of the solution changed from colorless to light yellow. The reacted solution was dialyzed against deionized water in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, and then freeze-dried to obtain a pale-yellow powder.

RBH was synthesized according to literature methods [25,26]. Briefly, 1.2 g of RB and 30 mL of absolute ethanol were added to a 100 mL three-necked flask and stirred until completely dissolved. 2 mL of hydrazine hydrate was slowly added dropwise in a magnetic stirring water bath under stirring at 80 °C under reflux. After heating under reflux at 80 °C for 2 h, the solution changed from dark red to transparent light orange. After cooling to room temperature, the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation. Then, 50 mL of 1 M hydrochloric acid and 70 mL of 1 M sodium hydroxide were added successively to adjust the pH of the solution to 11–12. The solution was vacuum filtered to obtain a pale pink precipitate, which was vacuum dried at room temperature to obtain 0.8 g of RBH. ESI-MS: m/z, calcd: 456.25, found: 457.35 ([M + H] +). 1H NMR (500 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 7.96-7.90 (m, 1H), 7.45 (dd, J = 6.0, 2.8 Hz, 2H), 7.14-7.06 (m, 1H), 6.49-6.40 (m, 4H), 6.29 (dd, J = 8.9, 2.6 Hz, 2H), 3.62 (s, 2H), 3.34 (q, J = 7.1 Hz 8H), 1.17(t, J = 7.0 Hz 12H).

Different concentrations of Hg2+ or Cu2+ were added to the B-CDs@RBH solution (0.5 mg/mL for B-CDs, 2 μM for RBH) and made up to 2.5 mL with 0.01 M pH 7 HEPES buffer (containing 5% acetonitrile), fluorescence spectra were recorded with an Edinburgh FS5. For ion selectivity experiments, replace Hg2+ or Cu2+ by adding other metal ions (including Mg2+, Ba2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+and Cr3+).

Tap water is taken from local domestic water at Henan university. The lake water is taken from Tingqin Lake, Jinming Campus of Henan University. All water samples were centrifuged and filtered through 220 nm microporous filters and stored in 4 °C refrigerators for later use.

Different concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ were dropped into the B-CDs@RBH solution, followed by taking fluorescent color photos by smartphone (iPhone XR, APPLE Inc.) under a 365 nm UV light. Color RGB values were procured through a smartphone software (color extraction, Xiyu Qian).

After immersing the filter paper strips in 0.01 pH 7 HEPES buffer solution (5% acetonitrile) containing 0.5 mg/mL B-CDs and 2 μM RBH for 5 min, the filter paper strips were removed from the solution and air-dried. After exposing the filter paper strips containing B-CDs@RBH to an aqueous solution of metal ions for 5 min, the paper strips were removed from the aqueous solution and air-dried at room temperature.

A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM, pH = 7.4) in the atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. 500 μL of cells were placed in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h, and then mixed with 500 μL of B-CDs@RBH at different concentrations (the concentrations of B-CDs were 0, 0.5, 2, 3, 3.5, 7, 14 mg/mL, and RBH concentrations of 0, 2, 4, 6, 7, 14, 28 μM, respectively) were incubated with cells. Then treated with 10 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT for 4 h and 100 μL of DMSO for 10 min, and recorded the absorbance at 570 nm wavelength.

Cells were seeded at various concentrations of Hg2+ or Cu2+ (0–5 μM) for 30 min at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Subsequently, these cells with B-CDs@RBH (0.5 mg/mL and 2 μM for B-CDs and RBH, respectively) were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, and images were obtained by confocal microscopy. 4-day-old wild zebrafish were processed as above-mentioned at 28 °C and images were acquired through a fluorescent inverted microscope.

TEM analysis reveals that B-CDs were spherical and monodisperse with an average diameter of 1.8 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. S1a). The HRTEM image of the B-CDs displays a clear lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (102) plane of graphitic carbon (inserted in Fig. S1a). The broad diffraction peaks (2θ) of B-CDs are concentrated at 25°, demonstrating the amorphous nature of the carbon core [27] (Fig. S1b).

The RBH presents a wide weak absorption peak in the range of 400–600 nm, and has no fluorescence emission (Fig. S2a). The two characteristic absorption peaks at 210 and 341 nm of B-CDs are attributed to aromatic sp2 domains and the n-π* transition, respectively [28–31]. The strongest fluorescence emission occurs at 440 nm under the excitation of 355 nm (Fig. S2b). Furthermore, solid B-CDs exhibits bright blue fluorescence under UV light, which is attributed to the successful doping of Na+ (Fig. S3a) [32]. As shown in Fig. S3b, the characteristic absorption band of B-CDs FT-IR spectrum at 3436 cm−1 assigned to the N–H/O–H stretching vibration, and the characteristic peak at 1590 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of N–H or the stretching vibration of COO−, the peak of 1398 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C–N, and 1177 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O [33–37]. The XPS spectra of B-CDs are shown in Fig. S4a, B-CDs contains four elements C, N, O and Na. C 1s reveals three peaks at 284.8, 285.2 and 288.0 eV which are attributed to CC, C–N and CO, respectively (Fig. S4b) [38–42]. The N 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted to amino nitrogen with a binding energy of 399.5 eV (Fig. S4c) [43], and peaks at 530.6, 532.1 and 534.9 eV assigned to CO, C–OH and C–O (Fig. S4d). It is evident that the surface of B-CDs synthesized by the functional preservation strategy does contain the functional groups of the precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of vision detection sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Under the guidance of the modular design strategy, the bifunctional fluorescent probe B-CDs@RBH simultaneously realizes the visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by simply blending B-CDs and RBH which has a specific response to copper. The preparation of B-CDs@RBH and the operating mechanism for visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ are clarified in Scheme 1 . As the Hg2+ reaction site, the fluorescence of B-CDs was quenched in the presence of Hg2+, and the carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of B-CDs coordinate with Hg2+ to form non-fluorescent ground state complexes. From the fluorescence lifetime decay curve (Fig. 1 a), it can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs changes from 7.01 ns to 6.95 ns before and after the addition of Hg2+, which is barely affected by the presence of Hg2+, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Hg2+ and the excitation spectrum of B-CDs do not overlap significantly (Fig. S5). This indicates that the quenching of B-CDs is static quenching rather than inner filter effect (IFE) [44,45]. Therefore, the luminescence quenching observed is due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Hg2+ coordinates with the functional groups attach onto the surface of the B-CDs forming B-CDs–Hg2+ complexes, which upon photo excitation transfer electron from B-CDs to the positively charge Hg2+, resulting in a PET process. The fluorescence of B-CDs is restored by adding superfluous EDTA (50 μM) into the solution of B-CDs–Hg2+, further indicating that the surface functional group of B-CDs and Hg2+ chelate to form a non-fluorescent complex, and the fluorescence of B-CDs is quenching by PET process (Fig. S6a).

As can be seen from Fig. S6b, the zeta potential of B-CDs@RBH is higher than that of B-CDs. Combined with the abundant functional groups on the surface of B-CDs and the intermolecular forces between RBH/RB, we speculate that B-CDs and RBH/RB may form a stable composite system through electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bond and other intermolecular forces. RBH can be specifically hydrolyzed by Cu2+, leading to the ring-opening of the lactam structure to generate RB with a turn-on strong fluorescence [24]. Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of RB overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. 1c), which is the premise of FRET, in which the fluorescence energy of donor B-CDs is transferred to the receptor RB, and the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs is attenuated, procuring the wane and wax dynamic fluorescence behavior. The FRET process between B-CDs and RB is also confirmed by the fluorescence decay curve, in which the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs before and after the hydrolysis of RBH by Cu2+ to form RB are 7.01 ns and 1.51 ns respectively, indicating the dynamic energy transfer process between B-CDs and RB (Fig. 1d) [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal working conditions of bifunctional fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH value on the response of B-CDs and RBH was inspected. As shown in Fig. S7a, the blue fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is significantly weakened when the pH is less than 6, while it is hardly affected when the pH is greater than 6, which can be achieved by the protonation/deprotonation of amino and carboxyl groups to understand. Under the conditions of room temperature and neutral pH, the hydrolysis reaction of RBH can be completed rapidly in a few seconds, and metal ions will form hydroxide precipitation in an alkaline environment, which is not conducive to its sensing and subsequent bioimaging, so pH 7 was taken as the working pH. As shown in Figs. S7b–c, B-CDs has good temperature stability and ionic strength stability. Besides, the stability of B-CDs@RBH was systematically investigated, and the fluorescence intensity ratio of B-CDs@RBH changed tenderly within 60 min, manifesting its good photostability (Fig. S7d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of B-CDs@RBH to Hg2+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 2 and 5 μM Hg2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence at 440 nm, and 5 μM Cu2+ can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm, thus the concentrations of Hg2+ and F440/F440 0 as well as the concentrations of Cu2+ with F570/F440 have a good linear relationship concentration. The detection limits of Hg2+ and Cu2+ are 17.79 nM and 4.19 nM, respectively (LOD = 3Sd/K, Sd: standard deviation of eleven blank solutions; K: the slope of the standard curve). In the cause of verifying the recognition ability of B-CDs@RBH to Hg2+ and Cu2+, selectivity and anti-interference experiments were put into effect. When 50 μM Mg2+, Ba2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ were added to the probe solution, the fluorescence color and intensity have not changed conspicuously. In the presence of other interfering substances, the detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ has almost no effect. The above results indicate that the B-CDs@RBH has prominent selectivity and anti-interference ability (Fig. 3 ). In order to differentiate between Hg2+ and Cu2+, S2O3 2− and ATP were added as masking agents for Hg2+ and Cu2+ respectively to avoid interference between them at 440 nm (Fig. S8). Furthermore, relative to the other fluorescent probes of heavy metal (Table S1), the B-CDs@RBH displays certain better characteristics in bio-applications and on-site applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for the detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Color extraction is a smartphone application that can recognize fluorescent colors and extract RGB values. When 0–5 μM Hg2+ or Cu2+ was added, B-CDs@RBH exhibits blue attenuation or continuous fluorescence change from blue to orange under a handheld UV lamp. Photos were taken with smartphone and RGB values were read by color recognition software. The results indicate that Hg2+ concentration with B value, and Cu2+ concentration with R/G value have a good linear relationship. Therefore, a portable on-site detection method for Hg2+ and Cu2+ was established with the help of smartphone (Fig. 4
). Compared with the smartphone-assisted identification of Hg2+ and Cu2+ based on fluorescent paper strips, the identification accuracy of samples in deionized water is higher and is not affected by the spontaneous fluorescence of fluorescent paper strips (Fig. S9).

B-CDs@RBH was used to determine Hg2+ and Cu2+ in tap water and lake water. As shown in Tables S3–4, no Hg2+ and Cu2+ are found in lake water and tap water. By adding different concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ and repeating five times, we discover that the recoveries of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ranged from 95.8% to 106.3%. The experimental data reveals that the detection process has good reproducibility, and B-CDs@RBH can detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ in real environmental water samples. For smartphone-assisted on-site quantitative determination, 0.5 and 5 μM Hg2+ or Cu2+ were added to pretreated lake water samples containing B-CDs@RBH, respectively. Fluorescence photos of samples solution under 365 UV lamp were taken with a smartphone camera and RGB color values were extracted (Fig. S10). The results manifest that the smartphone-assisted method can handy determine Hg2+ and Cu2+ in environmental samples without instrumentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was used to detect the cytotoxicity of B-CDs@RBH. As shown in Fig. S11, with the increase of B-CDs@RBH concentration (B-CDs concentration is 0–3.5 mg/mL, RBH concentration is 0–7 μM), the cell survival rate is still above 95%, pointing that B-CDs@RBH has good application prospects in the field of biological imaging. Therefore, in vitro and in vivo imaging abilities of B-CDs@RBH were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5, the intensity of A549 cells in the blue channel decreased significantly with the increase in Hg2+ concentration. The intensity of the blue channel decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, while that of the orange channel gradually increased (Fig. 6 ). Exogenous Hg2+ can chelate with the functional groups on the surface of B-CDs, so the fluorescence of B-CDs can be quenched by static quenching effect, while Cu2+ can be hydrolyzed to open RBH to generate highly fluorescent RB, which connotate that B-CDs@RBH can image intracellular Hg2+ and Cu2+ in complex intracellular environments. The zebrafish imaging reveals the same trend as the intracellular imaging (Figs. S14–15). These results indicate that B-CDs@RBH can be used to visually monitor Hg2+ and Cu2+ in cells and in vivo.

In conclusion, a bifunctional fluorescent probe B-CDs@RBH was modularly synthesized by simply blending B-CDs and RBH and exhibited high performance to trace Hg2+ or Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish. More intriguingly, a smartphone application can be used to identify the RGB color value of the probe solution, providing simple, instrumentless on-site water sample visual analysis. Combined with its low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, it reveals a broad prospect in future biological applications and on-site determination of heavy metals in actual environmental samples.

Yunhao Zhou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiahui Shi: Formal analysis. Juan Ning: Data curation. Guizhen Hu: Formal analysis. Yanmei Zhou: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978069, 22278112), Henan Province Science and Technology Research Project (222102520026).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111232.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced electrochemiluminescence behavior of gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters and its sensing application for mercury (II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhai Q, Xing H, Zhang X, Li J, Wang E. Enhanced Electrochemiluminescence Behavior of Gold-Silver Bimetallic Nanoclusters and Its Sensing Application for Mercury (II). Anal Chem 2017;89:7788-7794. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.7b01897.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-colored carbon dot ratiometric fluorescent test paper based on a specific spectral energy transfer for semiquantitative assay of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu C, Ning D, Zhang C, Liu Z, Zhang R, Zhao J, et al. Dual-Colored Carbon Dot Ratiometric Fluorescent Test Paper Based on a Specific Spectral Energy Transfer for Semiquantitative Assay of Copper Ions. ACS Appl Mater Inter 2017;9:18897-18903. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.7b05827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence emission of polyethylenimine-derived polymer dots and its application to detect copper and hypochlorite ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang H, Dong X, Wang J, Guan R, Cao D, Chen Q. Fluorescence Emission of Polyethylenimine-Derived Polymer Dots and Its Application to Detect Copper and Hypochlorite Ions. ACS Appl Mater Inter 2019;11:32489-32499. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.9b09545.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu X, Jin H, Gao C, Gui R, Wang Z. Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites. Talanta 2017;162:65-71. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2016.10.015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection and speciation of mono- and di-valent copper ions with a dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng AQ, Zhao CX, Wang XJ, Shu Y, Wang JH. Simultaneous detection and speciation of mono- and di-valent copper ions with a dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe. Chem Commun 2020;56:15337-15340. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0cc06750d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inorganic nanocrystal-dynamic porous polymer assemblies with effective energy transfer for sensitive diagnosis of urine copper

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ma X, Yang Y, Ma R, Zhang Y, Zou X, Zhu S, et al. Inorganic nanocrystal-dynamic porous polymer assemblies with effective energy transfer for sensitive diagnosis of urine copper. Chem Sci 2020;11:12187-12193. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0sc04359a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carrot-derived carbon dots modified with polyethyleneimine and nile blue for ratiometric two-photon fluorescence turn-on sensing of sulfide anion in biological fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jin H, Gui R, Wang Y, Sun J.
